sentences.base_language: Thomas signed up for a cooking class at the culinary school, something he had wanted to do for years.
sentences.target_language: Thomas s'est inscrit à un cours de cuisine à l'école culinaire, quelque chose qu'il avait voulu faire depuis des années.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'inscrire à** = to sign up for, to enroll in: **s'est inscrit à** un cours (signed up for a class). **S'inscrire** is a pronominal verb that uses **être** in the passé composé: il **s'est** inscrit (masculine, no agreement needed as the reflexive pronoun is indirect here). Compare: **inscrire quelqu'un** (to register someone else — transitive, uses avoir): elle a **inscrit** son fils.
**Qu'il avait voulu faire** — plus-que-parfait expressing a desire that pre-existed the main action: **avait voulu** (had wanted). The plus-que-parfait (**avait** + past participle) here places the desire firmly before the enrollment. **Depuis des années** = for years (ongoing up to that point): note that in a past context, **depuis** + imparfait expresses a duration up to a past moment: il **voulait** le faire **depuis** des années would also be correct in narration.

sentences.base_language: When he arrived, the instructor — an experienced chef named Hélène — welcomed the group warmly.
sentences.target_language: À son arrivée, l'instructrice — une cheffe expérimentée prénommée Hélène — a accueilli le groupe chaleureusement.
sentences.grammarNote:
**À son arrivée** = upon his arrival — **à** + possessive + verbal noun, an elegant alternative to **quand il est arrivé** (when he arrived). This construction with **à** + noun of action (**arrivée, départ, annonce, lecture**) gives writing a formal, polished register. Compare: **à son départ** (upon his departure), **à l'annonce de la nouvelle** (upon the announcement of the news).
**Prénommée** = named, called — past participle of **prénommer** used as an adjective: une cheffe **prénommée** Hélène (a chef named Hélène). **Prénommer** refers specifically to the given name (**prénom**), while **nommer** is more general (to name, to appoint). **Cheffe** is the feminine form of **chef** in modern standard French: **une cheffe** (female chef/head). The spelling **cheffe** with double f is now endorsed by official language bodies.

sentences.base_language: She announced that they would make a traditional three-course meal completely from scratch.
sentences.target_language: Elle a annoncé qu'ils allaient préparer un repas traditionnel de trois plats entièrement faits maison.
sentences.grammarNote:
**A annoncé que** + indicatif — verbs of declaration (**annoncer, dire, affirmer, déclarer**) take the indicative in the subordinate clause, not the subjunctive: elle a annoncé **qu'**ils **allaient** préparer (she announced that they were going to prepare). Contrast with verbs of doubt or emotion that trigger the subjunctive: elle **craignait qu'**ils **ne sachent** pas (she feared they might not know).
**Allaient préparer** — **aller** + infinitif in the imparfait expresses the near future in a past context (futur dans le passé): they were going to prepare (viewed from the moment of her announcement). This is the past equivalent of the present near future: ils **vont** préparer (they are going to prepare — now) vs ils **allaient** préparer (they were going to prepare — then). **Faits maison** = homemade, from scratch — a fixed expression: un plat **fait maison** (a homemade dish).

sentences.base_language: Thomas had never cooked anything more complex than pasta, so he felt both excited and nervous.
sentences.target_language: Thomas n'avait jamais rien cuisiné de plus complexe que des pâtes, alors il se sentait à la fois enthousiaste et nerveux.
sentences.grammarNote:
**N'avait jamais rien cuisiné** — double negation with the plus-que-parfait: **ne… jamais… rien** = never… anything. In French, multiple negative words can combine with **ne**: **ne… jamais rien** (never anything), **ne… plus jamais** (never again), **ne… plus rien** (nothing anymore). The auxiliary and the past participle are split: **n'**avait **jamais rien** cuisiné. Note: **rien** functions as the direct object here.
**À la fois… et…** = both… and…: **à la fois** enthousiaste **et** nerveux (both excited and nervous). **À la fois** is the standard French equivalent of 'both' when linking two qualities or states: il était **à la fois** surpris **et** soulagé (he was both surprised and relieved). Compare: **et… et…** (both… and… — emphatic, literary): **et** surpris **et** soulagé. **À la fois** is more natural in everyday speech.

sentences.base_language: First, Hélène demonstrated how to hold a knife correctly to avoid cutting their fingers.
sentences.target_language: D'abord, Hélène a montré comment tenir un couteau correctement pour éviter de se couper les doigts.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Montrer comment + infinitif** = to show how to do: **a montré comment** tenir (showed how to hold). This construction introduces an indirect question in infinitive form when the subject of both clauses is the same (or general): **montrer comment** faire (show how to do it). When subjects differ, use **montrer comment** + subject + verb: il lui a montré **comment** elle devait tenir le couteau (he showed her how she should hold it).
**Pour éviter de + infinitif** = to avoid doing, in order not to do: **pour éviter de** se couper (to avoid cutting themselves). **Éviter de** + infinitive = to avoid doing: **éviter de** faire une erreur (avoid making a mistake). **Se couper les doigts** — pronominal verb with body part: **se couper** + article + body part (reflexive + definite article replaces possessive): se couper **les** doigts (cut one's fingers), not *couper ses doigts*. This pattern is standard with body parts after reflexive verbs.

sentences.base_language: She explained that most kitchen accidents happen because people do not respect the blade.
sentences.target_language: Elle a expliqué que la plupart des accidents de cuisine arrivent parce que les gens ne respectent pas la lame.
sentences.grammarNote:
**La plupart de + article + noun** = most of: **la plupart des** accidents (most accidents). **La plupart de** is always followed by a plural noun with a definite article: **la plupart des** gens (most people), **la plupart du** temps (most of the time — **du** = de + le). The verb after **la plupart de** + plural noun is plural: **la plupart des** accidents **arrivent** (most accidents happen). Compare: **la majorité de** (the majority of — similar construction but more formal).
**Parce que + indicatif** = because (stating a cause as fact): **parce que** les gens **ne respectent pas** (because people do not respect). **Parce que** gives the real, factual reason. Contrast with **comme** (since, as — placing the reason before the main clause): **comme** les gens ne respectent pas la lame, les accidents arrivent. **Respecter la lame** = to respect the blade — using **respecter** for a tool implies treating it with appropriate caution and skill.

sentences.base_language: Thomas practiced chopping onions and his eyes began to water almost immediately.
sentences.target_language: Thomas s'est entraîné à émincer des oignons et ses yeux ont commencé à larmoyer presque immédiatement.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'entraîner à + infinitif** = to practice doing, to train oneself to do: **s'est entraîné à** émincer (practiced chopping). **S'entraîner** (pronominal, takes **être**): il **s'est** entraîné (masculine, no agreement since the reflexive pronoun is indirect). **S'entraîner à** emphasizes deliberate, repeated practice — more focused than **pratiquer** (to practice in general). Compare: **s'exercer à** (to practice/drill — similar but slightly more formal).
**Émincer** = to slice thinly, to mince: **émincer** des oignons (slice the onions). A key cooking verb in French: **émincer** (slice thinly), **hacher** (chop/mince finely), **couper en dés** (dice), **ciseler** (chiffonade — cut into fine strips, especially herbs). **Larmoyer** = to water (of eyes), to weep slightly — from **larme** (tear): ses yeux ont commencé à **larmoyer** (his eyes started to water). More specific than **pleurer** (to cry) for the physical reaction to onions or irritants.

sentences.base_language: The other students laughed kindly, and Hélène told them it was a sign the onions were very fresh.
sentences.target_language: Les autres étudiants ont ri gentiment, et Hélène leur a dit que c'était signe que les oignons étaient très frais.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Leur a dit** — indirect object pronoun **leur** (to them): **dire** à quelqu'un takes an indirect object for the person addressed: **leur** a dit (told them). **Leur** (them — indirect, for plural) vs **lui** (him/her — indirect, for singular): il **lui** a dit (he told him/her), elle **leur** a dit (she told them). Never use **les** as indirect object with **dire**: *elle les a dit* is incorrect. **Dire que** + indicatif: **a dit que** c'**était** signe (told them it was a sign — reported speech shifts present → imparfait in the subordinate clause).
**C'était signe que + indicatif** = it was a sign that: **c'était signe que** les oignons **étaient** très frais. Note the use of **imparfait** in both clauses in reported speech: the original statement would have been **c'est signe que les oignons sont frais** (present), but in reported speech after a past main verb, both shift to imparfait. This concordance of tenses (**concordance des temps**) is a key feature of formal French.

sentences.base_language: For the main course, they prepared a slow-cooked stew with seasonal vegetables and tender meat.
sentences.target_language: Pour le plat principal, ils ont préparé un ragoût mijoté avec des légumes de saison et de la viande tendre.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Ragoût** (m.) = stew — a slow-cooked dish of meat and vegetables in a sauce. Key French cooking vocabulary: **ragoût** (stew), **pot-au-feu** (boiled beef and vegetables), **blanquette** (creamy white stew, typically veal), **bœuf bourguignon** (beef braised in red wine), **cassoulet** (slow-cooked bean and meat casserole). **Mijoté/e** = slow-cooked, simmered — past participle of **mijoter** used as adjective: un ragoût **mijoté** (a slow-cooked stew). **Mijoter** (to simmer, to slow-cook) is an important culinary verb.
**De la viande** — partitive article **de la** before a feminine singular uncountable noun: **de la** viande (some meat — an unspecified quantity). The partitive articles (**du, de la, de l', des**) are used before uncountable or unspecified quantities: **du** beurre (some butter), **de la** farine (some flour), **de l'**huile (some oil). After negation, all partitives become **de**: pas **de** viande, pas **de** beurre. After quantity expressions: un kilo **de** viande (a kilo of meat — **de**, not **de la**).

sentences.base_language: Hélène walked around the room, tasting each person's dish and offering quiet suggestions.
sentences.target_language: Hélène a fait le tour de la salle en goûtant le plat de chacun et en proposant des suggestions discrètes.
sentences.grammarNote:
**En + gérondif** (present participle) = while doing, by doing — the gérondif expresses simultaneous action or manner: **en goûtant… en proposant** (while tasting… while offering). The gérondif always has the same subject as the main verb. Form: **en** + present participle (stem of nous-form + **-ant**): **en goûtant** (while tasting), **en proposant** (while offering), **en marchant** (while walking). When listing two gérondifs, **en** must be repeated before each: **en** goûtant **et en** proposant.
**Faire le tour de** = to go around, to make a tour of: **a fait le tour de** la salle (walked around the room). A very common and versatile expression: **faire le tour du** monde (go around the world), **faire le tour de** la question (go over the whole question), **faire le tour des** invités (go around to all the guests). **Chacun/e** = each one, each person: **le plat de chacun** (each person's dish). **Chacun** is an indefinite pronoun, always singular: **chacun** a son avis (everyone has their own opinion).

sentences.base_language: Thomas accidentally added too much salt but Hélène told him it was an easy mistake to fix.
sentences.target_language: Thomas a mis trop de sel par mégarde, mais Hélène lui a dit que c'était une erreur facile à corriger.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Trop de + nom** = too much / too many of: **trop de** sel (too much salt). **Trop de** is used with both countable and uncountable nouns: **trop de** sel (too much salt — uncountable), **trop de** fautes (too many mistakes — countable). Compare: **trop** + adjective/adverb (too + adjective/adverb): c'est **trop** salé (it's too salty). After **trop de**, no article is used: **trop de** sel (not *trop du sel*).
**Par mégarde** = accidentally, inadvertently — a fixed adverbial phrase: **par mégarde** (by mistake, without meaning to). Compare synonyms: **accidentellement** (accidentally — more formal), **sans le faire exprès** (without meaning to — colloquial), **par erreur** (by mistake — neutral). **Facile à + infinitif** = easy to do: une erreur **facile à** corriger (an easy mistake to fix). This construction — adjective + **à** + infinitive — describes how something relates to the action: **difficile à** comprendre (hard to understand), **agréable à** regarder (pleasant to look at).

sentences.base_language: She showed him how to balance the flavors by adding a dash of lemon juice and a pinch of sugar.
sentences.target_language: Elle lui a montré comment équilibrer les saveurs en ajoutant un filet de jus de citron et une pincée de sucre.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lui a montré** — indirect object **lui** (to him): **montrer à quelqu'un** = to show someone: elle **lui** a montré (she showed him). **Montrer** takes a direct object (what is shown) and an indirect object (to whom): elle **lui** a montré **comment** faire (she showed him how to do it). The indirect object pronoun (**lui/leur**) precedes the conjugated verb in compound tenses: elle **lui** a montré (not *elle a montré lui*).
**En ajoutant** — gérondif expressing manner or means: **en ajoutant** un filet (by adding a dash). The gérondif here answers 'how?': she showed him by adding lemon. **Filet** (m.) in a culinary context = a dash, a thin stream: **un filet de** jus de citron (a dash of lemon juice), **un filet d'**huile d'olive (a drizzle of olive oil). **Une pincée de** = a pinch of: **une pincée de** sel (a pinch of salt), **une pincée de** sucre (a pinch of sugar) — from **pincer** (to pinch).

sentences.base_language: The aroma from the pot filled the kitchen and made everyone's mouth water.
sentences.target_language: Le parfum qui s'échappait de la casserole a envahi la cuisine et a fait saliver tout le monde.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'échapper de** = to escape from, to drift out of: **qui s'échappait de** la casserole (that was drifting from the pot). The relative clause **qui s'échappait** uses the imparfait because the escaping was ongoing background action (the aroma was continuously rising). Compare: **qui s'est échappé** (which escaped — a one-time completed event). **S'échapper de** is used for gas, steam, scent, and also for people or animals escaping: le prisonnier **s'est échappé** (the prisoner escaped).
**Faire + infinitif** causative: **a fait saliver** tout le monde (made everyone's mouth water — lit. made everyone salivate). The causative **faire** + infinitive expresses that the subject causes the action: **faire saliver** (make salivate), **faire rire** (make laugh), **faire réfléchir** (make think). When the causee is a noun, it follows the infinitive: a fait saliver **tout le monde**. When the causee is a pronoun, it precedes **faire**: ça **leur** a fait saliver la bouche (it made their mouths water — indirect pronoun because there is also a direct object).

sentences.base_language: By the time the food was ready, Thomas's arms were tired from all the stirring.
sentences.target_language: Au moment où le repas était prêt, les bras de Thomas étaient fatigués à force de remuer.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Au moment où + imparfait** = by the time, at the moment when: **au moment où** le repas **était** prêt (by the time the meal was ready). **Au moment où** introduces a temporal clause and takes the indicative. The imparfait here describes the simultaneous state. Compare: **quand** + passé composé (when — for a specific completed moment): **quand** le repas **a été** prêt (when the meal was ready — punctual). **Au moment où** + imparfait gives a more cinematic, simultaneous feel.
**À force de + infinitif** = from so much doing, by dint of: **à force de** remuer (from all the stirring). This construction expresses a result of sustained or repeated action: **à force de** remuer, ses bras étaient fatigués (from stirring so much, his arms were tired). Compare: **à force de** + noun: **à force de** travail (through hard work — with a noun instead of infinitive). Both are correct. **Remuer** = to stir, to mix — the standard culinary verb: **remuer** la sauce (stir the sauce), **remuer** la soupe (stir the soup).

sentences.base_language: For dessert, they made a simple fruit tart that required careful attention to detail.
sentences.target_language: Pour le dessert, ils ont réalisé une tarte aux fruits simple qui nécessitait une attention minutieuse aux détails.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Réaliser** in a culinary context = to make, to execute, to create: **ont réalisé** une tarte (made a tart). **Réaliser** in French has several meanings: (1) to realize/make/execute a project or dish (false cognate alert: it does NOT primarily mean 'to realize' in the sense of 'to understand'); (2) to realize/become aware of (**réaliser que** = to realize that — now accepted in standard French). In cooking, **réaliser une recette** (to execute a recipe) is more formal than **faire une recette**.
**Tarte aux fruits** — preposition with food toppings: **tarte aux** fruits (fruit tart), **tarte au** citron (lemon tart), **tarte à la** fraise (strawberry tart). The preposition **à** + definite article is used for pies and tarts to indicate the main ingredient/flavor: **tarte aux** pommes (apple tart), **gâteau au** chocolat (chocolate cake), **glace à la** vanille (vanilla ice cream). This **à + article** construction for flavors is a fundamental French food pattern.

sentences.base_language: Thomas felt a surge of pride when his tart came out of the oven perfectly golden.
sentences.target_language: Thomas a ressenti une montée de fierté quand sa tarte est sortie du four parfaitement dorée.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Est sortie** — **sortir** uses **être** in the passé composé: **est sortie** (came out — feminine agreement with **la tarte**). The past participle **sortie** agrees with the feminine subject **tarte**: **sortie** (f. singular). The 16 verbs that use **être** as auxiliary (the 'DR MRS VANDERTRAMP' verbs or the 'house of être' verbs) include **sortir, entrer, arriver, partir, rester, tomber**, etc. All pronominal verbs also use **être**.
**Parfaitement dorée** — adjective **dorée** agreeing with **tarte** (feminine): **dorée** (golden — f.). **Doré/e** = golden (colored), golden-brown — past participle of **dorer** used as adjective: une tarte **dorée** (a golden tart), du pain **doré** (French toast — lit. golden bread). In cooking, **faire dorer** = to brown, to make golden: **faire dorer** le poulet (brown the chicken). **Ressenti** — past participle of **ressentir** (to feel, to experience an emotion): **a ressenti** une montée de fierté (felt a surge of pride). **Ressentir** is more internal and emotional than **sentir** (to smell or to feel physically).

sentences.base_language: The class sat down together to eat the meal they had prepared, feeling a warm sense of accomplishment.
sentences.target_language: Le groupe s'est assis ensemble pour manger le repas qu'ils avaient préparé, avec un sentiment chaleureux d'accomplissement.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Qu'ils avaient préparé** — plus-que-parfait in a relative clause: **qu'ils avaient préparé** (that they had prepared). The plus-que-parfait (**avaient** + past participle) expresses an action completed before another past action: they had prepared it earlier, now they were eating it. The relative pronoun **que** (which/that — direct object) causes past participle agreement: **qu'ils avaient préparé** — **préparé** does not agree here because the direct object (**que** referring to **le repas**, masculine) precedes the verb with **avoir**, but with **avoir** the agreement is only obligatory when the direct object pronoun precedes: le repas **qu'**ils avaient préparé (agreement optional in practice for many speakers).
**S'asseoir** = to sit down — an irregular pronominal verb: **s'est assis** (sat down, masculine singular) — past participle **assis/assise**. Note: **être assis** (to be seated — state) vs **s'asseoir** (to sit down — action): il **est assis** (he is sitting/seated), il **s'est assis** (he sat down). **Assieds-toi / Asseyez-vous** (sit down — imperative). **S'asseoir** is one of the most irregular French verbs: je m'**assieds** / je m'**asseois** (I sit down — two accepted forms in the present).

sentences.base_language: Thomas discovered that cooking was more satisfying than he had expected.
sentences.target_language: Thomas a découvert que la cuisine était plus satisfaisante qu'il ne l'avait prévu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Qu'il ne l'avait prévu** — the expletive **ne** with a comparative: **plus satisfaisante qu'il ne l'avait prévu** (more satisfying than he had expected). In careful/literary French, comparisons with **plus, moins, mieux, autre, autrement** take an expletive **ne** in the subordinate clause — this **ne** is not a negation: **plus facile qu'on ne le croit** (easier than one thinks), **moins cher qu'il ne le craignait** (cheaper than he feared). The expletive **ne** is increasingly rare in spoken French but marks educated written style.
**L'avait prévu** — the pronoun **le** (it) anticipating or resuming the content of the main clause: **il ne l'avait prévu** (he had not foreseen it — **le** refers to 'the fact that it was so satisfying'). **Prévoir** (to foresee, to expect, to plan) — irregular verb: **prévoir** → je **prévois**, nous **prévoyons**, j'ai **prévu**. Past participle: **prévu** (foreseen, expected, planned). Compare: **s'attendre à** (to expect — to anticipate emotionally): **il ne s'y attendait pas** (he didn't expect it).

sentences.base_language: Hélène invited them all to return for the next class, which would focus on regional French cuisine.
sentences.target_language: Hélène les a tous invités à revenir pour le cours suivant, qui porterait sur la cuisine régionale française.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Les a tous invités** — placement of **tous** as a floating quantifier: **les a tous** invités (invited them all). **Tous** agrees with the masculine plural direct object **les** (them): **tous** (m. plural). The quantifier **tous/toutes** floats to a position after the auxiliary in compound tenses: elle **les a tous** invités (she invited them all). Compare: elle **nous a tous** surpris (she surprised us all). Note the agreement of the past participle **invités** with the preceding direct object pronoun **les** (masculine plural) when using **avoir**.
**Porterait sur** — conditional of **porter sur** = would focus on, would be about: **qui porterait sur** la cuisine régionale (which would focus on regional French cuisine). The conditional (**porterait** = **porter** + **-ait** ending) here expresses the future seen from a past perspective (futur du passé in reported speech): the original statement would have been **le cours portera sur** (the class will focus on — future indicative), but in reported speech after a past main verb, the future becomes the conditional. Compare: il a dit que le cours **porterait** sur (he said the class would focus on).

sentences.base_language: Thomas walked home with a full stomach and a new passion, already planning what he would cook the following weekend.
sentences.target_language: Thomas est rentré chez lui le ventre plein et avec une nouvelle passion, envisageant déjà ce qu'il cuisinerait le week-end suivant.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Le ventre plein** = with a full stomach — the structure [definite article] + [body part / abstract noun] + [adjective] expresses manner or accompanying state: **le ventre plein** (with a full stomach), **les mains vides** (empty-handed), **la tête haute** (with head held high), **le cœur léger** (light-hearted). This construction is characteristically French and avoids the need for **avec** + possessive: **le ventre plein** rather than *avec son ventre plein*.
**Ce qu'il cuisinerait** — conditional in indirect question: **envisageant ce qu'il cuisinerait** (planning what he would cook). **Cuisinerait** = conditional of **cuisiner**: he was planning what he **would** cook. In indirect questions after a past verb, the future becomes the conditional: he was planning what he **would** cook (originally: what he **will** cook). **Envisager de + infinitif** = to consider doing, to plan to do: **envisager de** partir (to consider leaving). Here **envisager** is used without **de** because it takes a direct object clause (**ce qu'il cuisinerait**) rather than an infinitive.
